knowledge.statement: Data modeling is the process of defining how data is structured, stored, and related. A good data model reflects the domain accurately, supports required queries efficiently, and evolves without destructive changes.

First Normal Form (1NF) requires that every column holds atomic (indivisible) values and each row is unique. Storing comma-separated lists in a column violates 1NF and makes queries and updates error-prone.
Second Normal Form (2NF) requires 1NF and that every non-key column depends on the entire primary key, not just part of it. Partial dependencies cause update anomalies in composite-key tables.

Third Normal Form (3NF) requires 2NF and that every non-key column depends directly on the primary key, not on another non-key column (no transitive dependencies). 3NF eliminates redundancy and update anomalies.

Denormalization intentionally introduces redundancy to improve read performance. It trades write complexity and risk of inconsistency for query speed. Only denormalize after profiling confirms normalization is the bottleneck.

Primary keys uniquely identify each row. Surrogate keys (auto-increment integer, UUID) are system-assigned and stable; natural keys (email, SSN) carry domain meaning but change and may not be unique globally.

UUIDs as primary keys are globally unique and safe to generate client-side, but v4 UUIDs are random, causing index fragmentation in B-trees. Prefer UUIDv7 or ULID (time-ordered) when using UUIDs as clustered keys.

Foreign keys enforce referential integrity at the database level. Always define foreign key constraints; relying solely on application logic to maintain consistency is fragile and breaks on bulk imports and direct DB access.

An index speeds up reads by creating an auxiliary data structure. Adding an index has a write cost (every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update the index). Index only columns used in WHERE, JOIN, or ORDER BY clauses.
A composite index (multi-column) is used when queries filter on multiple columns together. Column order matters: the index is used for the leftmost prefix of the defined columns (left-prefix rule).

A covering index includes all columns required by a query, allowing the DB to answer it from the index alone without reading table rows. Covering indexes eliminate the most expensive part of a table lookup (heap fetch).
Partial indexes index only rows matching a condition (e.g. WHERE deleted_at IS NULL). They are smaller and faster than full-column indexes when queries target a subset of rows.

OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) databases are optimised for many small read/write transactions. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) systems are optimised for complex aggregations over large datasets. Do not run analytics directly against a production OLTP database.

A star schema is the standard OLAP data warehouse model: a central fact table linked to dimension tables. Facts are numeric measurements (sales_amount); dimensions are descriptive attributes (product_name, customer_region).
A snowflake schema normalises dimension tables into sub-dimensions. It reduces storage redundancy compared to a star schema but requires more joins, increasing query complexity.

Soft deletes mark rows as deleted (deleted_at timestamp) rather than removing them. They preserve history and simplify rollback but complicate queries (every query must filter out deleted rows) and grow the table indefinitely.

An audit table (or temporal table) records every change to a row with who made it and when. It is preferable to soft deletes when you need full change history, not just the current and deleted states.

Temporal tables (bi-temporal modeling) track both when a fact was true in the real world (valid time) and when it was recorded in the database (transaction time). SQL:2011 standardized PERIOD and AS OF syntax.

NULL means unknown or inapplicable, not zero or empty string. NULLs complicate comparisons (NULL = NULL is false; use IS NULL), aggregations (COUNT(*) vs COUNT(col)), and outer joins. Use NOT NULL constraints where a value is always required.

CHECK constraints enforce domain rules at the database level (e.g. CHECK (price > 0), CHECK (status IN ('active','inactive'))). They are faster and more reliable than application-layer validation alone.

Unique constraints guarantee that no two rows have the same value(s) in the specified column(s). They implicitly create an index. Unique constraints differ from primary keys: a table can have multiple unique constraints and they allow NULLs.
Schema migrations should be version-controlled alongside application code. Tools like Flyway, Liquibase, and Alembic manage migration history and ensure migrations run exactly once and in order.

Never modify a migration that has already been applied to production. Once a migration is deployed, it is immutable. Corrections require a new migration. Editing an applied migration breaks the migration history.

Large table migrations (adding a NOT NULL column, building a new index) must be done online to avoid locking the table for the duration of the operation. PostgreSQL's CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY and ALTER TABLE … ADD COLUMN with a default are key tools.

JSONB columns in PostgreSQL store semi-structured data that can be indexed and queried. Use them when a subset of rows needs variable attributes. Do not use JSONB as a substitute for proper schema design when the structure is stable.

Sharding partitions data horizontally across multiple databases. It solves write scalability that a single node cannot handle. Sharding adds enormous operational complexity; exhaust vertical scaling and read replicas first.

Read replicas serve read traffic, offloading the primary write node. They are the first scalability lever after indexing. Replication lag means replicas may return stale data; route consistency-critical reads to the primary.

Connection pooling (PgBouncer, HikariCP) manages a shared set of database connections. Each application instance opening its own unbounded pool exhausts DB connection limits. Pool at one layer; avoid double-pooling.
The N+1 query problem occurs when code fetches N parent records and then executes one query per record to fetch related data, totalling N+1 queries. Fix with JOIN, subquery, or ORM eager loading.

EXPLAIN ANALYZE shows the query execution plan the database chose and the actual runtime statistics. Always run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on slow queries before adding indexes; the planner may already have a good path.

Optimistic locking detects write conflicts at commit time using a version number or timestamp. It is suitable for low-contention scenarios and avoids holding locks. Pessimistic locking (SELECT FOR UPDATE) holds a row lock for the transaction duration.

ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) are guarantees provided by relational databases. Atomicity ensures all-or-nothing transactions; Isolation prevents dirty reads between concurrent transactions.

Isolation levels (READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE) trade concurrency for consistency. Most applications use READ COMMITTED; SERIALIZABLE eliminates all anomalies but reduces throughput significantly.

A surrogate key generated by the database (SERIAL, IDENTITY, SEQUENCE) is predictable and sequential; exposing it in APIs leaks row counts and enables enumeration attacks. Use a UUID or hashid in public-facing APIs.

Table partitioning divides a large table into smaller physical segments by range, list, or hash. Postgres declarative partitioning enables query pruning (only scanning relevant partitions) and faster data retention management (DROP PARTITION).

Full-text search in PostgreSQL (tsvector/tsquery) handles natural-language search with stemming and ranking. For complex search requirements (faceting, fuzzy matching, multi-language), a dedicated search engine (Elasticsearch, Typesense) is more appropriate.

pgvector extends PostgreSQL with a vector column type and approximate nearest-neighbour indexes (HNSW, IVFFlat). It enables semantic similarity search directly in the database, avoiding a separate vector store for moderate-scale applications.

Entity-Relationship (ER) diagrams visualise tables, columns, and relationships (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many). Diagram the schema before writing migration code to catch modelling errors when they are cheap to fix.

Many-to-many relationships require a junction table (association table) with foreign keys to both parent tables. The junction table may carry its own attributes (e.g. a 'role' column on a user_projects table).

Row-level security (RLS) in PostgreSQL allows policies that restrict which rows a database role can see or modify. It enforces multi-tenancy at the database layer, preventing data leaks even if application code is buggy.

Data types should be as narrow as possible: use SMALLINT not BIGINT for a column that never exceeds 32,767; use DATE not TIMESTAMP when time is irrelevant. Narrower types save storage, fit more data in cache, and improve index density.

Storing money as NUMERIC (DECIMAL) prevents the floating-point rounding errors of FLOAT and DOUBLE. Financial calculations must use exact arithmetic; rounding errors compound across millions of transactions.

Always store timestamps in UTC. Convert to the user's local timezone only for display. Storing local times in the database causes ambiguous data at DST transitions and makes date arithmetic across timezones incorrect.

Event sourcing stores a sequence of immutable events as the primary data store. The current state is derived by replaying events. It provides a complete audit trail and enables temporal queries, but adds replay complexity.

CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separates the write model (commands) from the read model (queries). Read models are optimised for their specific queries and may be denormalised. Write and read models evolve independently.

Database seeding populates a database with known, consistent test data for local development and integration testing. Seeding scripts should be idempotent and produce deterministic data for reproducible test runs.

Graph databases (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune) model data as nodes and edges. They excel at relationship-heavy queries (social networks, recommendation engines, access control graphs) that require many JOINs in a relational database.

Time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) are optimised for sequential, timestamped data with high write throughput and range queries over time. Use them for metrics, sensor data, and logs rather than a general-purpose RDBMS.

Document databases (MongoDB, Firestore) store self-describing JSON documents, enabling flexible schemas. They are a natural fit when data is hierarchical, each entity has different attributes, and the read pattern matches the document structure.

The single-table design pattern in DynamoDB stores multiple entity types in one table, differentiated by partition key and sort key conventions. It supports efficient access patterns but requires knowing query patterns at design time.

Database vacuuming (PostgreSQL autovacuum) reclaims storage from dead rows left by updates and deletes. MVCC creates dead rows on every update; without regular vacuuming, tables bloat and query planner statistics go stale.

The RETURNING clause in INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE statements retrieves modified rows without a separate SELECT. It reduces round trips and eliminates race conditions between a write and the subsequent read.

Materialised views precompute and cache the result of a complex query. They speed up heavy analytical reads but require explicit refresh. Use for reports and aggregations that do not need real-time freshness.

Schema documentation should be stored alongside the schema: table purpose, column semantics, enum value meanings, and non-obvious constraints. Without documentation, every column named 'type' or 'status' becomes a mystery.
